reasoning: All four correctly diagnose induced demand and prescribe congestion pricing; they separate on the disconfirming test and structural depth. C is strongest: explicit arithmetic refutation of the population story (15-17 percent one-time capacity bump vs roughly 4 percent population over two years), a Popperian too-easy-to-vary critique, a calibrated 85 percent confidence, a list of what it would verify, the peripheral-bypass boundary case, and a concrete honesty metric. B matches the systems framing (Meadows leverage hierarchy, reinforcing vs balancing loops, goal shift) and the selective-skeptic test but its disconfirmation stays qualitative. D is rigorous and concrete (Duranton-Turner 2011, clean implementation list) but does less systems work and less steelmanning of the population co-factor. A is sound and well-calibrated on elasticity ranges but stays first-order with the thinnest disconfirming test.
